TABLE 5: Physicians’ advice to patients who exercised during the first and second
trimesters regarding the duration and/or intensity of aerobic and strength-
conditioning exercise during the third trimester
Advice regarding maximum heart rate and perceived exertion
Participants were surveyed on their recommendations regarding maximum heart rate (bpm)
and level of perceived exertion during an exercise session (Table 6). More than half (54%; n=37)
recommend pregnant patients limit exercise based upon heart rate, most commonly 121-140
bpm (25%; n=17) or 141-160 bpm (24%; n=16), whereas 46% ( n=31) do not advise an upper
heart rate limit. Also, more than half of physicians (59%; n=40) believe perceived exertion
should be somewhat hard, while one-fourth (27%; n=18) advise no limit to exercise based upon
perceived exertion.
2018 McGee et al. Cureus 10(8): e3204. DOI 10.7759/cureus.3204 7 of 19
